moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that controls the critical central square e5 while keeping our options open—we haven't committed to the center yet. The knight on f3 supports multiple plans: we can build toward e4 later, play c4 to control the queenside, or even fianchetto on the kingside. This move is named after 19th-century grandmaster Johannes Zukertort and remains a favorite of players who prefer positional maneuvering over forced tactical lines. By playing Nf3 first, we avoid early d4 commitments and maintain maximum flexibility.

Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop on b7, aiming to control the long diagonal and put pressure on our center—particularly the e4 square. This move tells us that Black wants a flexible, positional game rather than sharp tactical complications. By preparing b7-bishop pressure early, Black is also eyeing potential counterplay on light squares (especially e4) and keeping the a-file semi-open for potential rook activity. This is the Queenside Fianchetto, less forcing than meeting our Nf3 with e5, so we need to be ready to establish our own piece placement before Black completes their setup.
